findings.title: generate-screener.js writes screener.html with non-atomic writeFileSync
findings.description: generate-screener.js uses `fs.writeFileSync(args.out, html)` to write screener.html directly without the tmp+rename atomic pattern. Every other output-producing script in the repository (generate-modes-report, generate-dashboard, generate-diff-report, generate-methods-report) uses `writeFileAtomic`. The screener.html file can be several hundred KB; a SIGKILL mid-write truncates the file. On Karl's Windows+OneDrive box specifically, OneDrive sync can hold a read handle mid-write and corrupt the file even without a crash.
findings.mechanism: fs.writeFileSync opens the target file, truncates it, then writes bytes sequentially. A process kill between truncation and final flush leaves a zero-byte or partial screener.html. The gh-pages deploy step copies this partial file to _site/screener.html and pushes it.
findings.reproducer: Interrupt the GitHub Actions 'Generate Screener Dashboard' step mid-run (or trigger a step timeout). The next run's deploy will publish a broken screener.html until the job completes successfully.
findings.impact: Dashboard UI is partially broken or blank for the window between the failed write and the next successful run. Users see a corrupt HTML page deployed to GitHub Pages.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `fs.writeFileSync(args.out, html)` at line 3491 with `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('./lib/atomic-write.js'); writeFileAtomic(args.out, html);` — same pattern already used by generate-dashboard.js line 601.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots.js writes new NDJSON archive files with non-atomic writeFileSync
findings.description: When writing a brand-new monthly NDJSON archive file (the `else` branch at line 102-103), `fs.writeFileSync(ndjsonPath, lines)` is used directly. The append path (line 101) uses `fs.appendFileSync` — also non-atomic, but at least it does not truncate existing data. A SIGKILL during `writeFileSync` of a new file leaves a zero-byte or partial NDJSON. The post-write verify at line 114 runs AFTER the write and can detect the partial, preventing unlink of originals — but the corrupt NDJSON now persists on disk. On re-run, the existing-dates set is built from the corrupt NDJSON, treating parsed lines as present and potentially skipping re-append of those dates, further corrupting the archive.
findings.mechanism: fs.writeFileSync truncates then writes bytes. SIGKILL between truncation and final flush → zero-byte or partial file. Verify step reads the partial, logs a warning, skips unlink. On re-run: partial file is the existing archive; existingDates is read from corrupt lines; toWrite excludes any dates whose lines happened to parse; unlink of originals fires if toWrite is empty — permanently losing those vintages.
findings.reproducer: Run archive-old-snapshots.js on a large month (e.g., methods-history-archive/2026-04.ndjson) and kill the process mid-write of the first occurrence. The file is left partially written. On re-run, if the partial file has enough valid lines, some originals may be unlinked on a corrupt base.
findings.impact: Corrupt NDJSON in external-data/methods-history-archive/ or external-data/picks-history-archive/. Methods-history vintages from that month are permanently inaccessible from the archive unless the operator manually repairs the file.
findings.suggested_fix: Use writeFileAtomic for the overwrite path: `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('../lib/atomic-write.js'); writeFileAtomic(ndjsonPath, lines);` for the else branch (line 103). For the append path (line 101), read the full file, build the merged content in memory, and writeFileAtomic the result — NDJSON archives are typically small enough to hold in memory, and the atomic swap is the only correct approach when file integrity is required.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots.js append path has TOCTOU: concurrent runs double-write entries
findings.description: The append path reads the NDJSON file to collect existing dates (lines 85-88), then appends new lines (line 101), under no lock. If two concurrent workflow_dispatch runs (which get their own concurrency group per daily-pull.yml line 22 and thus do not serialize) both archive the same month simultaneously, both read the same existing-dates set before either appends. Both compute the same toWrite set. Both append the same lines. The archive ends up with 2x entries for those dates. Downstream consumers (backtest, method-effectiveness) parse all lines without dedup, silently double-counting those vintages.
findings.mechanism: Run A: read(existingDates) → {2026-04-01,...}. Run B: read(existingDates) → {2026-04-01,...}. Run A: append(new-dates). Run B: append(same-new-dates). Archive has duplicates. method-effectiveness.js iterates all NDJSON lines without date-dedup → counts each method pass/fail twice for duplicated dates.
findings.reproducer: Trigger two manual workflow dispatches 30 seconds apart on a day when a monthly archive rotation is due. Check the resulting NDJSON for duplicate `date` values.
findings.impact: Methods-history or picks-history vintages are double-counted in walk-forward-perf and method-effectiveness, producing inflated alpha metrics and phantom signals.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a read-build-writeAtomic pattern: read the full existing NDJSON, parse all lines into a Map keyed by date, add/replace new entries, write the full Map back via writeFileAtomic. The atomic rename serializes concurrent writes naturally (last writer wins, which is correct for date-keyed content).

findings.title: detect-changes.js uses Date.now() for today, ignoring RUN_DATE_UTC consensus date
findings.description: Every other snapshot-* script (snapshot-picks.js line 203, snapshot-methods-history.js line 53, scripts/snapshot-score-history.js line 160) uses `process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC || new Date().toISOString().slice(0,10)` to agree on a single frozen run date. detect-changes.js uses `const today = new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10)` unconditionally without checking RUN_DATE_UTC. The workflow freezes RUN_DATE_UTC at job start (daily-pull.yml lines 53-57), but if the Yahoo pull crosses UTC midnight, detect-changes runs with tomorrow's date while methods-history and picks-history were written with the frozen prior date. This causes every METHOD_PASS_LOST/GAINED event to be stamped with tomorrow's date, misaligning alert-state.json with the methods-history vintage it describes.
findings.mechanism: RUN_DATE_UTC=2026-05-18 frozen at job start. Yahoo pull runs ~3h, finishing 01:00 UTC 2026-05-19. detect-changes.js runs after the pull, reads new Date() → '2026-05-19'. Writes lastChanged:'2026-05-19' for today's method-flip events. methods-history vintage is named 2026-05-18.json. pick-diff's findMethodFile lookup for date '2026-05-18' finds no matching alert-state entry (all entries are stamped '2026-05-19').
findings.reproducer: Run the workflow starting at 23:30 UTC. The pull takes 90-165 minutes. detect-changes runs with tomorrow's date.
findings.impact: Alert-state method-change events are timestamped one day ahead. The 30-day pruning in saveState() (detect-changes.js line 116) uses lastChanged for cutoff, so events dated tomorrow will not be pruned on schedule. Pick-diff vintage lookups may produce empty diffs.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 310 to: `const today = process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC || new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10);` — mirrors the fix already applied to every other snapshot-* script.

findings.title: detect-changes.js file filter admits _manifest-full.json as a real stock snapshot
findings.description: The file filter at line 389 is `f !== '_manifest.json'`. snapshots/ also contains `_manifest-full.json` (written by pull-yahoo.js, gitignored per .gitignore line 46). snapshot-methods-history.js fixed this exact bug at Tag 227c-1 (line 66: `!f.startsWith('_')`), as did snapshot-picks.js (line 30), generate-modes-report.js, and generate-screener.js (Tag 220). detect-changes.js was never updated to use startsWith('_'). `_manifest-full.json` passes the filter, is loaded as a stock object, and Runner.evaluateStock() runs on the manifest shape `{pulled_at, n_ok, ...}`. ticker falls back to `file.replace(/\.json$/, '')` = `_manifest-full`. All method evaluations produce computable results on the pseudo-object (or errors that are caught and wrapped as non-computable). The phantom ticker is written into alert-state.methodState and methodHistory on every run. The orphan-cleanup in main() (line 329) checks only for dead method-ids, not for phantom ticker names — `_manifest-full` is never pruned because it has a fresh lastChanged on every run.
findings.mechanism: fs.readdirSync returns `_manifest-full.json`. Filter at line 389 passes it (only `_manifest.json` is excluded by exact match). JSON.parse succeeds. ticker = `_manifest-full`. detectMethodDiffs runs with empty prevMethods → generates METHOD_PASS_NEW events for every method. Phantom ticker is written to alert-state.methodState. saveState() pruning only drops entries with lastChanged>30d — a ticker refreshed every run is never 30d stale, so it accumulates indefinitely.
findings.reproducer: Run detect-changes.js on any snapshots/ directory that contains _manifest-full.json (present after every Yahoo pull). Check alert-state.json for a `_manifest-full` key in methodState.
findings.impact: alert-state.json grows unbounded with phantom method entries for `_manifest-full`. METHOD_PASS_LOST/GAINED events for the phantom ticker may fire on Discord (if webhook is configured). The phantom entry bloats the 21.6 MB alert-state.json on every run. methodHistory sidecar also grows with phantom entries.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 389 to: `const files = fs.readdirSync(args.snapshots).filter(f => f.endsWith('.json') && !f.startsWith('_'));` — identical to the fix already applied in snapshot-methods-history.js, snapshot-picks.js, generate-modes-report.js, and generate-screener.js.

findings.title: watchlist-cli.js save() hand-rolls tmp+rename without writeFileAtomic durability guarantees
findings.description: watchlist-cli.js save() writes via `fs.writeFileSync(tmp, ...); fs.renameSync(tmp, PATH)` — a hand-rolled tmp+rename that predates lib/atomic-write.js. This pattern misses every durability guarantee from Tag 230c-1: (1) no fsync on the tmp file before rename, so a power-loss/OS crash between writeFileSync and renameSync can corrupt watchlist.json on POSIX; (2) no Windows EPERM/EBUSY retry for the rename, so on Karl's Windows+OneDrive machine an AV scanner or OneDrive sync-client scan mid-rename raises EPERM and leaves the tmp file stranded while watchlist.json remains the stale version; (3) no parent-directory fsync. watchlist.json is the universe source of truth consumed by every pull-yahoo run.
findings.mechanism: writeFileSync writes bytes to tmp (no fsync). renameSync raises EPERM on Windows because OneDrive holds a read handle on watchlist.json during sync. The rename fails and throws; the tmp file is stranded. The cmdAdd/cmdRemove caller does not catch this error, so the user sees an unhandled exception and their operation is silently lost.
findings.reproducer: Run `node watchlist-cli.js add FOO` on Karl's Windows box with OneDrive sync active while OneDrive scans watchlist.json. The rename will fail with EPERM.
findings.impact: Watchlist add/remove/import operations fail silently on Windows+OneDrive. The user must re-run the command. In the worst case, OneDrive holds a lock long enough for the user to assume success, leading to a missing ticker in the next pull.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace save() body with: `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('./lib/atomic-write.js'); writeFileAtomic(PATH, JSON.stringify(wl, null, 2));` — writeFileAtomic already handles EPERM retry (RENAME_RETRY_DELAYS_MS), fsync, and parent-dir fsync.

findings.title: pull-prices-bulk.js _safeMergeAndWrite hand-rolls tmp+rename without writeFileAtomic
findings.description: _safeMergeAndWrite() in pull-prices-bulk.js uses `fs.writeFileSync(tmp, ...); fs.renameSync(tmp, HISTORY_PATH)`. Same missing durability as F-SM-006: no fsync before rename, no EPERM retry for Windows+OneDrive, no parent-dir fsync. prices/history.json grows to tens of MB and is read by walk-forward-perf.js, backtest-pass-vs-fail.js, and macro-regime.js for alpha calculations. _safeMergeAndWrite is called once per ticker inside a for-loop over 25 stocks: if EPERM fires mid-loop, subsequent stocks still update the in-memory `out` dict but all their _safeMergeAndWrite calls also fail, losing up to 25 price series per batch invocation.
findings.mechanism: writeFileSync writes tmp (no fsync). renameSync raises EPERM on Windows while OneDrive scans history.json. Merge is lost. The in-memory `out` object still accumulates new series, but all writes for the remaining stocks in the batch also fail. The file is left as the pre-batch version; all batch progress is discarded.
findings.reproducer: Run `node pull-prices-bulk.js 0 25` on Karl's Windows+OneDrive box with OneDrive syncing history.json. Observe EPERM failures.
findings.impact: Historical price series for up to 25 tickers per batch run are silently lost. Walk-forward alpha calculations use stale or missing price data, producing phantom performance numbers.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the tmp+rename pattern in _safeMergeAndWrite() with `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('./lib/atomic-write.js'); writeFileAtomic(HISTORY_PATH, JSON.stringify(merged, null, 2));` — same pattern already used in pull-historical-prices.js line 198.

findings.title: aktienfinder-import.js writes aktienfinder.json without atomic write, silently loses history on corrupt re-read
findings.description: aktienfinder-import.js uses `fs.writeFileSync(outPath, JSON.stringify(merged, null, 2))` at line 38 with no tmp+rename. A process kill or power-loss during this write truncates aktienfinder.json. The load path at line 35 silently resets to `{}` on parse error via bare `catch (e) {}`, so a corrupt write causes all previously imported scores to be silently discarded on the next import invocation. The merge at line 37 is `Object.assign(existing, data)` where existing starts as `{}` — all prior scores are replaced by only the new CSV's data.
findings.mechanism: writeFileSync truncates then writes. SIGKILL between truncation and completion → corrupt JSON. Next invocation: load existing → JSON.parse throws → `existing = {}` (silent catch). merge → only new CSV scores. All prior accumulated scores from previous imports are permanently lost.
findings.reproducer: Kill node during aktienfinder-import.js execution. Run again with any CSV. The resulting aktienfinder.json contains only the new CSV's scores.
findings.impact: All previously imported Aktienfinder scores (from prior runs of the import script) are permanently discarded. Methods or screener tabs relying on these scores revert to null/absent until a full re-import is done manually.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `const { writeFileAtomic } = require('./lib/atomic-write.js'); writeFileAtomic(outPath, JSON.stringify(merged, null, 2));` Also consider surfacing the parse error rather than silently swallowing it (change `catch (e) {}` to `catch (e) { console.error('Warning: could not parse existing aktienfinder.json, starting fresh:', e.message); }`.

findings.title: score-history/ will exceed GitHub 50 MB soft cap as universe grows to 25k tickers
findings.description: The .gitignore comment (lines 57-65) estimates score-history at 4147 tickers × 4.5 KB ≈ 18.7 MB and sets the 50 MB alert threshold at 6000 tickers. MAX_UNIVERSE is now 25000 (daily-pull.yml line 117, raised from 13000 in Tag 227a), and the watchlist already has 15734+ stocks. snapshot-score-history.js writes a per-ticker JSON for every stock with any computable score. Most stocks in the 25k universe have at least pbScore (revenue growth × gross margin formula) which is computable for any stock with those two metrics. At 25000 tickers × 4.5 KB = 112 MB — more than double GitHub's 50 MB soft cap and over the 100 MB per-file hard limit. The mitigation mentioned in the comment ('cap entries to 14 for non-pick tickers') is not implemented anywhere in the code; all tickers get MAX_ENTRIES=30.
findings.mechanism: snapshot-score-history.js skips writing only when ALL of {hgScore, qcScore, pbScore, hgTier, qcTier} are null (line 171-173). The pbScore formula (line 41-65) requires only revenueGrowthYoY and grossMargin — two common metrics. As the rolling daily pull eventually covers all 25k tickers (each ticker is visited every ~7 days at PULL_CONCURRENCY=8 and 25k universe), score-history grows to near universe size.
findings.reproducer: Let the pipeline run for 7+ days with MAX_UNIVERSE=25000 and observe score-history/ size growth via git ls-files --debug | grep -c 'score-history'.
findings.impact: score-history/ exceeds 50 MB, triggering GitHub push warnings. Above GitHub's file tree size limits, the push fails, stopping the daily commit. score-history/ state is no longer committed and the dashboard permanently loses ΔScore/sparkline rendering.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement the mentioned mitigation: only write MAX_ENTRIES=30 for tickers appearing in any active mode pick list, and MAX_ENTRIES=14 (or 7) for non-pick tickers. Or add a separate gitignore for non-pick score-history entries and rebuild from methods-history on demand.

findings.title: score-history schema mismatch silently discards all accumulated history on next schema bump
findings.description: readHistoryFile() at lines 110-114 resets entries to [] when parsed.schemaVersion !== SCHEMA_VERSION. SCHEMA_VERSION is hardcoded to 1. A future schema bump to 2 (to add, say, a pbTier field) will silently discard all 30 accumulated entries for every ticker on the next run. There is no migration path, no backup of old entries, and no operator warning beyond the per-ticker `::warning::` log line. At 25k tickers, 30-entry reset means the dashboard's ΔScore and sparklines will show zero history for up to 30 calendar days after the bump.
findings.mechanism: SCHEMA_VERSION changes from 1 to 2 in a commit. Next run: readHistoryFile sees schemaVersion=1 for every ticker on disk → returns {entries:[]}. appendAndPrune writes a single-entry history. All prior accumulated data is permanently discarded unless a separate backup is made.
findings.reproducer: Change SCHEMA_VERSION to 2 and run a daily pull. Inspect any score-history/*.json — it will contain only today's entry.
findings.impact: Dashboard shows no Δ7d score badges and empty sparklines for all tickers for up to 30 calendar days after the schema bump, creating a sustained user-visible regression with no automatic recovery.
findings.suggested_fix: Before resetting, write a bulk backup of old entries to external-data/score-history-backup-v<old_version>.ndjson (gitignored). Alternatively implement a migration function that maps old schema shape to new shape. At minimum document the data-loss risk in the SCHEMA_VERSION bump procedure.

findings.title: _buildFirstSeenMap rejects cache when any mode key is missing, forcing O(90-file) full rebuild
findings.description: _buildFirstSeenMap() at line 157 validates the cache with `modes.every(m => typeof cached[m] === 'object')`. If any one of the three mode keys is absent from the cache (e.g., TURNAROUND was added after the cache was first built, or was disabled for one run causing its key to be absent), the entire cache is rejected and a full rebuild is triggered by reading every picks-history/*.json vintage file. At --picks-keep-days 90 (Tag 233a), this is 90 vintage files (~65 KB each = ~5.8 MB) read and parsed serially, adding ~1-2s per run. More critically: the validation also fails when `cached['TURNAROUND']` is `null` or `undefined`. If a run creates the cache while TURNAROUND was disabled and the mode loop skipped initialization, the key may be absent in the written cache — causing full rebuild on every subsequent run until the cache is manually deleted or a mode toggle triggers a full rebuild that writes a complete cache.
findings.mechanism: mode.enabled=false → the `for (const modeId of ['HYPERGROWTH', 'QUALITY_COMPOUNDER', 'TURNAROUND'])` loop at line 289 executes but writes `result.modes[modeId] = []` (line 234). The cache-update loop at line 289-296 iterates modes and sets firstSeenMap[modeId] = {} if absent. So the cache should always have all three keys after a full run. BUT: if the cached value loaded from disk is `null` (not `{}`), `typeof null === 'object'` is `true` in JavaScript — so null values would actually pass validation. The real risk is an absent key, which can occur if the cache was written by an older version of snapshot-picks.js that did not have one of the three modes.
findings.reproducer: Manually delete TURNAROUND key from picks-history/_first-seen.json. Run snapshot-picks.js. Observe full rebuild from all vintage files.
findings.impact: Performance degradation (serial read of 90 vintage files) per run when cache is invalidated. If the vintage files have been partially archived to NDJSON (gitignored), the rebuild may produce an incomplete firstSeenMap, silently losing firstSeenAt timestamps for tickers in archived vintages.
findings.suggested_fix: In _buildFirstSeenMap, instead of rejecting the entire cache when a mode key is missing, initialize the missing mode to {} and merge from the disk cache for the present modes: only trigger full rebuild if the cache is structurally corrupt (not an object at all). This makes the cache self-healing for mode additions.

findings.title: Concurrent workflow_dispatch runs produce conflicting _first-seen.json with wrong firstSeenAt dates
findings.description: snapshot-picks.js reads _first-seen.json at startup (line 196), evaluates ~3300 stocks over several minutes, then writes the updated cache at line 299. Two concurrent workflow_dispatch runs (each gets its own concurrency group per daily-pull.yml line 22) can both read the cache before either write. Each run computes firstSeenAt for new tickers from its own RUN_DATE_UTC. Last writer wins: the second run's writeFileAtomic overwrites the first run's cache entirely. Tickers that the first run saw for the first time on its date (e.g., 2026-05-18) will now show firstSeenAt from the second run's date (e.g., 2026-05-19) in the surviving cache, silently misreporting ticker entry dates.
findings.mechanism: Run A (RUN_DATE_UTC=2026-05-18): read cache → evaluate → discover new ticker FOO with firstSeenAt=2026-05-18 → write cache. Run B (RUN_DATE_UTC=2026-05-19, overlapping): read cache (same as Run A's read — before A's write completes) → evaluate → FOO now appears in B's universe too, with firstSeenAt=2026-05-19 → write cache (overwrites A's). FOO's firstSeenAt is permanently 2026-05-19.
findings.reproducer: Trigger two manual workflow dispatches 1 minute apart on consecutive UTC days.
findings.impact: weeksOnList for newly-entered tickers is off by ~1 week for the overlap window. Dashboard weeksOnList badges show incorrect tenure.
findings.suggested_fix: Re-read _first-seen.json immediately before writing (inside the cacheUpdated branch) and merge the in-memory additions into the just-read disk version, rather than writing the cache that was loaded at startup. This is a read-modify-write operation but writeFileAtomic's EPERM retry handles the Windows rename contention.

findings.title: methods/_helpers.js module-level median caches are process-global singletons, unsafe for parallel tests
findings.description: _sectorMediansCache, _autoMediansCache, and _rollingMediansCache are module-level null-initialized variables (lines 85-88). Once populated they are reused for the entire process lifetime. The test-only hook _setMediansForTest/_restoreMediansFromTest modifies this global state. If a test runner invokes tests concurrently (e.g., with --parallel flag), two tests that both call _setMediansForTest() will interfere: test A sets _autoMediansCache={}, test B simultaneously calls effectiveThreshold() and reads the blank cache, getting the wrong threshold for its fixture. The existing tests run sequentially, so this does not fire today — but the pattern is a latent hazard for any future parallel test execution or worker-pool usage.
findings.mechanism: Test A: _setMediansForTest({},{},{}) → _autoMediansCache={}. Test B: effectiveThreshold(stock, 'rule-of-40', 15) → _loadAutoMedians() returns {} → falls through to default. Test A: _restoreMediansFromTest(saved). Test B got wrong threshold and may produce wrong pass/fail.
findings.reproducer: Run tag28-tests.js with a Jest --runInBand=false or similar parallel flag. Fixture-hash tests that call _setMediansForTest will intermittently produce wrong hashes.
findings.impact: Intermittent CI failures in parallel test execution. Stale thresholds in any multi-step single-process orchestration (not the current workflow, but a risk for future refactors).
findings.suggested_fix: Document in the _setMediansForTest comment that it is not concurrency-safe and must not be used in parallel test execution. Alternatively, refactor the load functions to accept an override parameter rather than using global mutable state.

findings.title: pull-sec-xbrl.js writes individual XBRL JSON files with non-atomic writeFileSync
findings.description: pull-sec-xbrl.js at line 125 uses `fs.writeFileSync(filePath, res.body)` to write individual SEC XBRL JSON files to the gitignored external-data/sec-xbrl/ directory. A SIGKILL during a large XBRL write (some CIK files are several MB) leaves a truncated file. On the next pull, the 304-not-modified check (`prior.lastModified`) will prevent a re-fetch: the manifest entry (written atomically via writeFileAtomic at lines 145/152) records the lastModified date as fresh. The corrupt file is used indefinitely until manually deleted.
findings.mechanism: writeFileSync truncates then writes. Kill between truncation and flush → zero-byte or partial XBRL file. Next pull: manifest has lastModified set → SEC returns 304 → manifest.fetchedAt updated → corrupt file stays. Methods that load the XBRL file get a JSON.parse error and fall back to no data — permanently, until the operator manually deletes the corrupt file and re-pulls.
findings.reproducer: Kill pull-sec-xbrl.js mid-write of a large XBRL file. Check the manifest entry and verify the file is partial. Run pull-sec-xbrl.js again and observe no re-fetch.
findings.impact: XBRL-dependent methods (institutional-ownership-13f.js) silently produce no data for the affected ticker until the XBRL cache entry is manually cleared.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace line 125 with `writeFileAtomic(filePath, res.body)` — lib/atomic-write.js writeFileAtomic accepts Buffer data via the `Buffer.isBuffer(data)` branch at line 147.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots.js uses Date.now() for cutoff, ignoring RUN_DATE_UTC frozen date
findings.description: archiveDirByDate() computes the cutoff date from `new Date()` at lines 47-50 rather than from `process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC`. If the pipeline runs near UTC midnight and the archive step executes after the day boundary, the cutoff is based on a different date than the frozen date used for the vintage filenames by snapshot-methods-history.js and snapshot-picks.js. At the current --methods-keep-days 90 and --picks-keep-days 90 this is a low-risk edge case, but it is an inconsistency with the established RUN_DATE_UTC consensus pattern. If keepDays is ever reduced to a small value (e.g., 7), today's vintage could be immediately archived on a midnight-crossing run.
findings.mechanism: RUN_DATE_UTC=2026-05-18 frozen at job start. archive-old-snapshots.js runs at 02:05 UTC 2026-05-19. cutoff = 2026-05-19 - keepDays. If keepDays=7, cutoff = 2026-05-12. methods-history/2026-05-18.json: 2026-05-18 >= 2026-05-12 → kept (correct). But if keepDays=1, cutoff = 2026-05-18. 2026-05-18 >= 2026-05-18 → kept (also correct by >=). Only dangerous if cutoff = 2026-05-19 and today's file is named 2026-05-18 — that requires keepDays=0.
findings.reproducer: Set --methods-keep-days 0 and run archive-old-snapshots.js immediately after midnight crossing. Today's vintage (named with the frozen date) would be archived.
findings.impact: Low severity given current 90-day window. Relevant only if keepDays is reduced to single digits in future tuning.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass `process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC || new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10)` as the base date to archiveDirByDate() from main(), so the cutoff is consistent with the rest of the pipeline.

files_skipped: alert-state.json (18M+ tokens, schema and behavior inspected via detect-changes.js loadState/saveState code)
picks-history/ directory (runtime-only, inspected via snapshot-picks.js code)
methods-history/ directory (runtime-only, inspected via snapshot-methods-history.js code)
score-history/ directory (runtime-only, inspected via scripts/snapshot-score-history.js code)
external-data/aktienfinder.json (runtime data, schema examined via aktienfinder-import.js)
methods/sector-medians-compute.js (sector-medians generation, not a state writer)
